Step 2: Let's denote the intersection of the angle bisectors of \(\angle ABC\) and \(\angle ADC\) and the line \(AC\) by point \(I\). This point \(I\) is the incenter of the triangles \(ABC\) and \(ADC\) formed by the angle bisectors.

Step 3: Since \(I\) is the incenter of \(\triangle ABC\), it is equidistant from \(AB\), \(BC\), and \(CA\). Similarly, since \(I\) is the incenter of \(\triangle ADC\), it is equidistant from \(AD\), \(DC\), and \(CA\).

Step 4: The perpendiculars from \(D\) to \(BC\), \(CA\), and \(AB\) form right angles with these lines. Since \(I\) is equidistant from the sides of both triangles, the distances from \(I\) to the lines \(BC\), \(CA\), and \(AB\) are equal. This implies that the feet of the perpendiculars \(P\), \(Q\), and \(R\) from \(D\) to \(BC\), \(CA\), and \(AB\) form a configuration where the segments \(PQ\) and \(QR\) are symmetric with respect to the line \(AC\).

Step 5: Therefore, \(PQ = QR\) if and only if the distances from \(I\) to the lines \(BC\) and \(AB\) are equal, which is true if and only if the angle bisectors of \(\angle ABC\) and \(\angle ADC\) and the line \(AC\) intersect at a single point.

Step 6: Conversely, if the angle bisectors of \(\angle ABC\) and \(\angle ADC\) and the line \(AC\) intersect at a single point, then \(I\) is equidistant from the sides of both triangles, which implies that \(PQ = QR\).

Thus, we have shown that \(PQ = QR\) if and only if the angle bisectors of \(\angle ABC\) and \(\angle ADC\) and the line \(AC\) intersect at a single point.

\(\boxed{PQ = QR \text{ if and only if the angle bisectors of } \angle ABC \text{ and } \angle ADC \text{ and the line } AC \text{ intersect at a single point.}}\)

\boxed{PQ = QR \text{ if and only if the angle bisector of } \angle ABC, \text{ the angle bisector of } \angle ADC, \text{ and the line } AC \text{ intersect at a single point.}}
